steps.prompt: Is there an explicit verb in this sentence?

hints.yes — 'Pax' is a verb: 'Pax' is a noun meaning 'peace' — it is not a verb
hints.yes — 'vobis' is a verb: 'Vobis' is a pronoun in the dative — it is not a verb
hints.yes — both words are verbs: Neither word is a verb — both are nominal forms

steps.prompt: What verb is understood, and what would its mood be?
steps.target: subjunctive 'sit' (jussive: 'may peace be / let peace be')
choices: subjunctive 'sit' (jussive: 'may peace be / let peace be')
steps.reveal: Pax vobis [sit] — jussive subjunctive expressing a wish/blessing

hints.indicative 'est' (peace is): While 'est' is possible, the greeting formula typically implies a wish — subjunctive 'sit'
hints.imperative 'esto' (be!): 'Esto' is possible but the jussive subjunctive is more natural for a blessing
hints.infinitive 'esse' (to be): An infinitive cannot be the main verb of an independent clause

steps.prompt: Principal parts of the understood verb?

hints.eo, ire, ii, itum: These are the parts of 'eo' (to go)
hints.do, dare, dedi, datum: These are the parts of 'do' (to give)
hints.fio, fieri, factus sum, —: These are the parts of 'fio' (to become)

hints.vocative: Vocative would be for direct address — 'Pax' is the subject of the implied verb

hints.1st declension: 1st declension has genitive in -ae — 'pacis' ends in -is
hints.2nd declension: 2nd declension has genitive in -i — 'pacis' ends in -is
hints.4th declension: 4th declension has genitive in -us — 'pacis' ends in -is

hints.ablative: 'Vobis' can be ablative, but without a preposition here, dative of advantage is more natural
hints.accusative: Accusative of 'vos' is 'vos' — 'vobis' is dative or ablative
hints.genitive: Genitive of 'vos' is 'vestrum/vestri' — 'vobis' is dative here

steps.target: dative of advantage / dative of possessor
choices: dative of advantage / dative of possessor
steps.reveal: Peace [be] to you — the peace is for your benefit

hints.dative of purpose: Dative of purpose names a goal or function — 'vobis' names the recipient of peace
hints.dative of reference: Dative of reference is close, but 'advantage' better captures the blessing sense
hints.dative of agent: Dative of agent is used with passive periphrastic — this is a simple wish formula

steps.prompt: This formula 'Pax vobis' reflects which Hebrew greeting?

hints.Baruch HaShem (blessed be the Name): 'Baruch HaShem' means 'blessed be the Name' — 'Pax vobis' = 'Shalom aleichem'
hints.Hallelujah (praise the Lord): 'Hallelujah' means 'praise the Lord' — 'Pax vobis' is the Latin of 'Shalom'
hints.Amen (so be it): 'Amen' means 'so be it' — 'Pax vobis' translates the Semitic greeting 'Shalom'

steps.prompt: What is the grammatical name for this type of sentence (no explicit verb)?
steps.target: nominal sentence (verbless sentence with implied copula)
choices: nominal sentence (verbless sentence with implied copula)

hints.infinitive phrase: An infinitive phrase would need an infinitive — there is none here
hints.ablative absolute: Ablative absolute requires ablative case — 'Pax' is nominative
hints.imperative sentence: An imperative sentence has an imperative verb — the implied verb here is subjunctive

steps.prompt: In what liturgical context is 'Pax vobis' used?
steps.target: greeting of the bishop/priest at Mass
choices: greeting of the bishop/priest at Mass
it is not used in liturgy

hints.the consecration: The consecration uses 'Hoc est corpus meum' — 'Pax vobis' is a greeting
hints.the final blessing only: 'Pax vobis' can be used at the beginning of Mass by a bishop, not only at the end
hints.it is not used in liturgy: 'Pax vobis' is used in the Roman Rite as a greeting, especially by bishops
